title: Maggots in Dog Wounds: Emergency Care & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Finding maggots in your dog's wound (myiasis) is a high-level medical emergency. Learn why immediate vet care is required and how triage photos help.
content_html:
What is Myiasis?

Myiasis is the medical term for an infestation of fly larvae, commonly known as maggots, in living tissue. This typically occurs when flies are attracted to an open wound, skin infection, or even matted fur soiled with urine or feces. The flies lay eggs that hatch into larvae, which then feed on the dog's tissue.

Is This a Medical Emergency?

Yes, finding maggots in a wound is considered a high-level emergency. The urgency level is High. Maggots can cause rapid and extensive tissue damage in a very short amount of time. Beyond the physical destruction of the skin, the larvae can release enzymes and toxins that lead to secondary bacterial infections, severe inflammation, and potentially life-threatening shock or sepsis if left untreated.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary triage team. A photo allows the staff to assess the severity and depth of the infestation before you even arrive, helping them prioritize your pet's care upon entry. It also serves as a clinical record of the wound's state before the area is shaved and cleaned, ensuring no part of the infestation is overlooked during treatment.

faqs.question: Should I try to remove the maggots myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should not attempt to remove them at home. It is often impossible to remove all larvae without professional tools, and many over-the-counter products are toxic to dogs. Immediate veterinary debridement is required.

faqs.question: Is myiasis painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: Yes, myiasis is extremely painful and distressing for pets. Your veterinarian will provide appropriate sedation and pain management during the cleaning process to ensure your dog's comfort.

faqs.question: What is the typical treatment for maggots in a wound?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves sedation, clipping the fur, manual removal of all larvae, thorough flushing of the wound, and a course of antibiotics and anti-inflammatory medications to prevent infection.
